title: High-volume watery diarrhea that persists with fasting, with profound weakness

chart.cc: Months of large-volume watery diarrhea and severe weakness
chart.oneLiner: 54F with mild hypertension presents with ~5 months of progressively profuse, watery, tea-colored, non-bloody diarrhea (often several liters a day) that does NOT stop when she fasts, now with profound muscle weakness, cramps, palpitations, lightheadedness, and ~12 lb weight loss.

opening: I've had this watery diarrhea for months — it just pours out of me even when I haven't eaten anything, and now I'm so weak I can barely stand.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 54-year-old woman, looks tired, mildly dry, sitting on the stretcher feeling weak and a bit shaky. Cooperative, frightened by how much fluid she is losing and how weak she has become. Good historian.

HPI: About 5 months of WATERY, voluminous, NON-bloody, NON-greasy diarrhea — she describes it as 'pouring water,' often several large watery movements totaling what feels like liters a day, sometimes tea/weak-tea colored. The KEY feature (reveal when asked about fasting or whether food matters): it does NOT stop when she stops eating — she tried a full day of clear liquids and even fasting and the watery stool kept coming, which surprised her. No abdominal pain to speak of, no urgency-relieved cramps, just the volume. Over the same months: profound MUSCLE WEAKNESS and cramps (legs give out climbing stairs), intermittent PALPITATIONS, lightheadedness/near-fainting on standing, increased thirst, and ~12 lb unintentional weight loss. She has noticed occasional facial FLUSHING but it is minor and not the dominant feature. NO blood or mucus in stool, NO nocturnal sweats/fevers, NO recent antibiotics, NO recent travel, NO sick contacts, NO new foods, and (reveal only if asked) NO laxative or 'detox/colon-cleanse' or weight-loss product use at all. NO heartburn or epigastric ulcer-type pain (pertinent negative against gastrinoma — only if asked).
PMH: mild essential hypertension. No diabetes, no thyroid disease, no known pancreatic disease, no prior GI surgery. MEDS: amlodipine 5 mg daily ONLY. Specifically: NO laxatives, NO PPIs, NO antibiotics recently, NO herbal/OTC weight-loss or 'cleanse' products (she is emphatic she has not used any laxatives if asked). ALLERGIES: none. SOCIAL: retired teacher, lives with her spouse, rare alcohol, never smoked, no recreational drugs; she is NOT trying to lose weight and denies any eating-disorder or surreptitious laxative behavior if gently asked. FAMILY: no known endocrine tumors, no MEN syndrome, no pituitary/parathyroid/pancreatic tumors that she knows of (only if asked). ROS: weakness, cramps, palpitations, thirst, constipation never; no flushing-triggered wheeze; no bone pain; no kidney stones known.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: tired, mildly ill-appearing, looks volume-depleted; resting on the stretcher.
- Vitals: BP 98/60, HR 108, RR 18, 36.8°C, SpO2 98% RA. Orthostatic if checked: drops to 84/54 with HR rise to 124 on standing (significant orthostasis).
- Skin/mucous membranes: dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor (dehydration). Occasional faint facial flush, not dramatic.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular; no murmur.
- Lungs: clear.
- Abdomen: soft, NON-tender, no rebound/guarding, no organomegaly palpable, normoactive-to-increased bowel sounds; no mass appreciated on routine exam.
- Neuro/MSK: diffuse proximal muscle weakness and hyporeflexia consistent with profound hypokalemia if motor strength/reflexes tested; no focal deficit.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the single most important clue (that the diarrhea PERSISTS WITH FASTING) unless asked whether fasting/not eating changes it or whether food affects it.
- Do NOT volunteer the laxative/cleanse-product negative, the family history, or the flushing unless asked.
- Dehydration signs, orthostasis, and the muscle weakness/hyporeflexia are revealed ONLY if the relevant exam/maneuver is performed.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.BMP: Na 138, K 2.4 (profound hypokalemia), Cl 116, HCO3 14, BUN 34, Cr 1.6 (prerenal AKI from volume loss), glucose 122. Anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO3) = 138 − (116 + 14) = 8 — a NORMAL/low anion gap NON-anion-gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis from stool bicarbonate loss, NOT a high-gap acidosis.
labs.Calcium: Total calcium 11.6 mg/dL (HYPERcalcemia), albumin 3.9 (corrected ~11.7), ionized calcium elevated — fits VIPoma (hypercalcemia) and argues AGAINST the hypocalcemia of malabsorption.
labs.Magnesium: Mg 1.5 mg/dL (mildly low), phosphate low-normal — coexisting electrolyte losses that worsen the hypokalemia and arrhythmia risk.
labs.StoolStudies: Stool volume >1 L/day and PERSISTS during a supervised 48-hour fast (secretory pattern). Stool osmotic gap LOW (~30 mOsm/kg; gap = 290 − 2×[stool Na + stool K]) — confirms a SECRETORY (not osmotic) diarrhea. Stool laxative screen (including bisacodyl/senna/magnesium) NEGATIVE; stool cultures, C. difficile toxin/PCR, and ova & parasites NEGATIVE; fecal fat not increased.
labs.VIP: Fasting plasma vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP) MARKEDLY ELEVATED — clinches a VIP-secreting neuroendocrine tumor (WDHA / Verner–Morrison syndrome). Plasma chromogranin A also elevated (neuroendocrine marker). Gastrin NORMAL (argues against Zollinger–Ellison/gastrinoma); fasting glucose only mildly high.
labs.Imaging: CT/MRI pancreas: a ~3 cm hypervascular mass in the pancreatic TAIL with a few small hypervascular liver lesions; 68Ga-DOTATATE PET shows somatostatin-receptor-avid uptake in the pancreatic mass and hepatic lesions — a well-differentiated pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia at ~108 bpm with the electrocardiographic signature of profound hypokalemia (K 2.4): prominent U waves, flattened/inverted T waves, ST depression, and an apparent long QT/QU interval; the PR is normal. A torsades-de-pointes / ventricular-arrhythmia substrate driven by her secretory potassium losses (her hypercalcemia tends to shorten the true QT, so the long QU is the hypokalemia/U-wave fusion).
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: sinus tachycardia with prominent U waves, flattened T waves, ST depression and apparent long QU (severe hypokalemia). Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing sinus tachycardia with prominent U waves, flat T waves and ST depression of severe hypokalemia
searchTerms: severe hypokalemia flat T waves ST depression 12 lead
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 54-year-old woman with months of high-volume secretory diarrhea, profound weakness, and palpitations has an EKG drawn after her potassium returns at 2.4 mmol/L.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Clear lung fields; normal cardiomediastinal silhouette; no free air, effusion, or infiltrate — a normal screening film in a dehydrated patient with a metabolic, not pulmonary, problem.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: clear lungs, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette, no acute process. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph with clear lungs and no acute process
searchTerms: clear lungs normal cardiomediastinal silhouette radiograph
cxr.clinicalVignette: A volume-depleted woman with chronic secretory diarrhea has a screening chest film during her metabolic workup.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, give your differential for this chronic high-volume watery diarrhea (note your leading diagnosis). Explicitly state how you would distinguish a SECRETORY from an OSMOTIC diarrhea, and name the must-not-miss secretory causes.
steps.rubric: VIPoma / WDHA (Verner–Morrison) secretory diarrhea — leading (leading,7). Competing must-not-miss: SURREPTITIOUS laxative abuse / factitious diarrhea — must be actively excluded (CANT-MISS,4). Carcinoid syndrome (other secretory neuroendocrine tumor) (CANT-MISS,3). Zollinger–Ellison / gastrinoma and other islet-cell tumors; medullary thyroid carcinoma (core,3). Distinguishes secretory vs osmotic: secretory diarrhea PERSISTS WITH FASTING and has a LOW stool osmotic gap (<50), osmotic diarrhea STOPS with fasting and has a HIGH gap (>100) (core,5). Exclude chronic infection incl. C. difficile / Giardia, and other endocrine causes (hyperthyroidism) (core,3). PENALTY: anchoring on infectious gastroenteritis or ordering empiric antibiotics without recognizing a months-long secretory, fasting-persistent picture (-3); failing to consider factitious/laxative diarrhea (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: a VIPoma causing WDHA (Watery Diarrhea, Hypokalemia, Achlorhydria — Verner–Morrison) syndrome — a high-volume SECRETORY diarrhea. The key discriminator is that her diarrhea PERSISTS with fasting and is huge in volume. To distinguish SECRETORY from OSMOTIC diarrhea: a secretory diarrhea continues during a fast and has a LOW stool osmotic gap (<50 mOsm/kg; gap = 290 − 2×[stool Na + K]), whereas an osmotic diarrhea STOPS with fasting and has a HIGH gap (>100). Must-not-miss competitors I must actively exclude: surreptitious laxative abuse / factitious diarrhea (stool laxative screen), carcinoid syndrome and other secretory neuroendocrine tumors (gastrinoma/Zollinger–Ellison, medullary thyroid carcinoma). Also exclude chronic infection (C. difficile, Giardia) and hyperthyroidism. The profound hypokalemia, dehydration, and weight loss raise the urgency.

criticalActions.item: Surreptitious laxative abuse / factitious diarrhea — actively exclude

coreActions.item: Gastrinoma / Zollinger–Ellison and other islet-cell tumors; medullary thyroid carcinoma
Distinguishes secretory (persists with fasting, low stool osmotic gap) from osmotic (stops with fasting, high gap)
Exclude chronic infection (C. difficile, Giardia) and hyperthyroidism

penalties.item: Anchors on infectious gastroenteritis / empiric antibiotics despite a months-long fasting-persistent secretory picture

mustNotMiss: Surreptitious laxative abuse / factitious diarrhea — actively exclude
Gastrinoma / Zollinger–Ellison and other islet-cell tumors; medullary thyroid carcinoma

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: BMP — capture the hypokalemia, non-anion-gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis, and AKI (CANT-MISS,3). Stool studies: measure stool volume + OSMOTIC GAP and document persistence during a supervised fast; stool LAXATIVE screen; stool cultures/C. difficile/O&P to exclude infection (CANT-MISS,4). Fasting plasma VIP (and chromogranin A) — the confirmatory secretory-tumor marker (CANT-MISS,3). Serum calcium and magnesium (multi-system electrolytes; hypercalcemia supports VIPoma) (core,2). Cross-sectional pancreatic imaging (CT/MRI) ± DOTATATE PET to localize the tumor (core,2). Gastrin and TSH to exclude gastrinoma and thyroid disease (core,1). PENALTY: ordering empiric antibiotics or sending a colonoscopy first without measuring stool osmotic gap / VIP, or repleting nothing while working up (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG and CXR; BMP (hypokalemia, hyperchloremic non-anion-gap acidosis, AKI); serum calcium and magnesium; stool studies — stool volume and stool osmotic gap with documentation that diarrhea persists during a supervised fast, a stool laxative screen, plus stool cultures, C. difficile toxin/PCR, and ova & parasites to exclude infection and factitious causes; fasting plasma VIP and chromogranin A as the confirmatory markers; gastrin and TSH to exclude gastrinoma and thyroid disease; and CT/MRI of the pancreas ± 68Ga-DOTATATE PET to localize the neuroendocrine tumor.

criticalActions.item: Stool studies: volume + osmotic gap, fasting test, laxative screen, infectious panel

coreActions.item: Gastrin and TSH to exclude gastrinoma and thyroid disease

penalties.item: Empiric antibiotics or colonoscopy-first without stool osmotic gap / VIP, or repleting nothing while working up

requiredTests: Stool studies: volume + osmotic gap, fasting test, laxative screen, infectious panel
optionalTests: Gastrin and TSH to exclude gastrinoma and thyroid disease
contraindicatedTests: Empiric antibiotics or colonoscopy-first without stool osmotic gap / VIP, or repleting nothing while working up
orderSensitiveActions: Stool studies: volume + osmotic gap, fasting test, laxative screen, infectious panel

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG, drawn after her potassium returned at 2.4 mmol/L. Describe your findings and tie them to her presentation.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia (~108) with prominent U waves, flattened/inverted T waves, ST depression, and an apparent long QT/QU — the electrocardiographic signature of PROFOUND HYPOKALEMIA. CREDIT: identifies sinus tachycardia and the U waves / flat T waves / ST depression of hypokalemia (6); recognizes the apparent long QU as a torsades-de-pointes / ventricular-arrhythmia substrate (5); links the picture to her secretory potassium (and magnesium) losses and the need for urgent telemetry + K/Mg repletion (4). PENALTY: calling the tracing normal or missing the hypokalemia / arrhythmia risk (-3).
keyFindings: sinus tachycardia with prominent U waves and flattened/inverted T waves
ST depression and apparent long QT/QU interval of severe hypokalemia
links it to secretory potassium/magnesium loss and torsades risk
criticalFindings: Prominent U waves with flat/inverted T waves, ST depression and apparent long QU on a background of sinus tachycardia — the signature of profound hypokalemia (K 2.4) and a torsades substrate.
commonMistakes: mistaking the U waves for T waves and missing the hypokalemia
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia at ~108 bpm with prominent U waves, flattened/inverted T waves, ST depression, and an apparent long QT/QU interval. This is the classic footprint of PROFOUND HYPOKALEMIA (K 2.4) — driven by her high-volume secretory potassium losses and worsened by the low magnesium — and it is a torsades-de-pointes / ventricular-arrhythmia substrate that explains her palpitations. (Her coexisting hypercalcemia tends to shorten the true QT, so the long interval is the U-wave/QU fusion of hypokalemia.) It mandates continuous telemetry with urgent, careful potassium AND magnesium repletion.

criticalActions: Prominent U waves with flat/inverted T waves, ST depression and apparent long QU on a background of sinus tachycardia — the signature of profound hypokalemia (K 2.4) and a torsades substrate.
coreActions: sinus tachycardia with prominent U waves and flattened/inverted T waves
ST depression and apparent long QT/QU interval of severe hypokalemia
links it to secretory potassium/magnesium loss and torsades risk

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the hypokalemia and arrhythmia risk

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = clear lungs, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette, no acute process — a normal screening film; the problem is metabolic, not pulmonary. CREDIT: recognizes a normal film (6); states the pertinent negatives (no infiltrate, effusion, or free air) and that it does not explain the diarrhea/electrolyte derangement (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology on a clear film (-3).
keyFindings: clear lung fields, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette
no acute process — screening film, problem is metabolic not pulmonary
criticalFindings: Normal chest film: clear lungs, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette, no acute process.
commonMistakes: inventing an infiltrate or effusion on a clear film
steps.idealAnswer: Clear lung fields with a normal cardiomediastinal silhouette and no infiltrate, effusion, or free air — a normal screening chest film. It confirms there is no pulmonary cause and reinforces that this is a metabolic/secretory problem, not a thoracic one.

criticalActions: Normal chest film: clear lungs, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette, no acute process.
coreActions: clear lung fields, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette
no acute process — screening film, problem is metabolic not pulmonary

penalties.label: calls the study abnormal / invents pathology

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: The stool osmotic gap is low and the diarrhea persisted through a supervised fast, the laxative screen and infectious studies are negative, fasting plasma VIP is markedly elevated with high chromogranin A and a normal gastrin, and imaging shows a hypervascular DOTATATE-avid pancreatic-tail mass with small liver lesions. What is your diagnosis, and what did you rule out? Name three mechanisms by which this tumor produces its electrolyte and acid–base picture.
steps.rubric: VIPoma (pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor) causing WDHA / Verner–Morrison syndrome (6). Cites the markedly elevated fasting VIP with a SECRETORY profile (low stool osmotic gap, persists with fasting) and DOTATATE-avid pancreatic mass; explicitly excludes factitious laxative diarrhea (negative laxative screen), infection (negative stool studies), and gastrinoma (normal gastrin). Names three mechanisms: (1) VIP-driven intestinal secretion of water and electrolytes → massive watery diarrhea and dehydration/AKI; (2) profound fecal POTASSIUM loss → hypokalemia; (3) fecal BICARBONATE loss → non-anion-gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis; (also achlorhydria from VIP-inhibited gastric acid, and tumor-associated hypercalcemia) (4).
steps.idealAnswer: VIPoma — a VIP-secreting pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor causing WDHA (Verner–Morrison) syndrome. It is confirmed by a markedly elevated fasting plasma VIP with high chromogranin A in the setting of a SECRETORY diarrhea (low stool osmotic gap that persists through a supervised fast) and a hypervascular, DOTATATE-avid pancreatic-tail mass with hepatic lesions. This rules out factitious/laxative diarrhea (negative laxative screen), chronic infection (negative cultures/C. difficile/O&P), and gastrinoma/Zollinger–Ellison (normal gastrin). Three mechanisms producing her electrolyte/acid–base picture: (1) VIP stimulates massive intestinal secretion of water and electrolytes, producing the high-volume watery diarrhea with dehydration and prerenal AKI; (2) profuse fecal potassium loss causes the profound hypokalemia (K 2.4) and muscle weakness; (3) fecal bicarbonate loss causes a non-anion-gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis (gap 8). Additional features: VIP-induced inhibition of gastric acid causes achlorhydria/hypochlorhydria (the 'A' of WDHA), and the tumor is associated with hypercalcemia.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including stabilization, definitive therapy, consults, and disposition/counseling?
steps.rubric: Per MGH neuroendocrine tumor / secretory diarrhea: aggressive IV fluid RESUSCITATION for the large secretory losses with close ins/outs (CANT-MISS,5); urgent, careful POTASSIUM repletion (with MAGNESIUM repletion first/alongside) on continuous telemetry given the EKG hypokalemia/torsades substrate (CANT-MISS,6); correct the non-anion-gap acidosis and address hypercalcemia as volume is restored (core,2); start a SOMATOSTATIN ANALOG (octreotide/lanreotide) to suppress VIP and control the secretory diarrhea (core,4); cross-sectional/DOTATATE staging with surgical resection of the pancreatic NET (curative if localized) and oncology/endocrine-surgery referral, with hepatic-directed therapy/PRRT for metastatic disease (core,4); admit (not discharge) the dehydrated, severely hypokalemic patient and arrange close follow-up; counsel on the diagnosis and that antidiarrheals/antibiotics alone will not control a hormone-driven secretory diarrhea (core,4). PENALTY: discharging or giving oral rehydration alone for a severely hypokalemic, volume-depleted patient (-4); repleting potassium without magnesium or without telemetry (-3); treating empirically as infectious/IBD with antibiotics or steroids and missing the secretory tumor (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Stabilize first: admit and give aggressive IV crystalloid resuscitation for the massive secretory fluid losses with strict ins/outs and serial electrolytes. Place on continuous telemetry and urgently but carefully replete POTASSIUM, repleting MAGNESIUM alongside (hypomagnesemia makes hypokalemia refractory) — her EKG shows a profound-hypokalemia/torsades substrate. Correct the non-anion-gap (hyperchloremic) metabolic acidosis as volume and potassium are restored, and treat the hypercalcemia with volume. Start a SOMATOSTATIN ANALOG (octreotide, then long-acting lanreotide) to suppress VIP and control the secretory diarrhea — antidiarrheals/antibiotics alone will not control a hormone-driven diarrhea. Stage with CT/MRI and 68Ga-DOTATATE PET and refer to endocrine surgery and oncology: surgical resection of the pancreatic-tail neuroendocrine tumor is potentially curative if localized; for hepatic metastases consider cytoreduction, hepatic-artery embolization, or peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT). Counsel the patient on the diagnosis and the plan, and arrange close oncology/endocrine follow-up — do NOT discharge this dehydrated, severely hypokalemic patient.

criticalActions.item: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation for large secretory losses with strict ins/outs
Urgent careful potassium (with magnesium) repletion on continuous telemetry

coreActions.item: Somatostatin analog (octreotide/lanreotide) to suppress VIP and control diarrhea
Staging + surgical resection of pancreatic NET; oncology/endocrine-surgery referral (PRRT/embolization if metastatic)
Admit, not discharge; counsel that antidiarrheals/antibiotics alone won't control a hormone-driven secretory diarrhea; close follow-up
Correct the non-anion-gap acidosis and hypercalcemia as volume/potassium are restored

penalties.item: Discharging or giving oral rehydration alone for a severely hypokalemic, volume-depleted patient
Repleting potassium without magnesium or without telemetry
Empiric antibiotics/steroids as if infectious/IBD, missing the secretory tumor

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 54F presents with ~5 months of high-volume, watery, NON-bloody secretory diarrhea that PERSISTS with fasting, plus profound muscle weakness, palpitations, dehydration, and weight loss. The diagnosis is a VIPoma causing WDHA (Verner–Morrison) syndrome — confirmed by a low stool osmotic gap that persists through a supervised fast, a markedly elevated fasting plasma VIP, and a DOTATATE-avid pancreatic-tail neuroendocrine tumor. It is reached by eliciting the fasting-persistence clue, distinguishing secretory from osmotic diarrhea, recognizing the multi-system metabolic derangement (profound hypokalemia, non-anion-gap acidosis, hypercalcemia, prerenal AKI), and excluding factitious laxative diarrhea, infection, carcinoid, and gastrinoma.
keyHistoryClues: High-volume watery diarrhea that does NOT stop with fasting (secretory pattern)
Profound muscle weakness, cramps, palpitations and orthostatic lightheadedness from potassium loss
Unintentional weight loss with minor, non-dominant facial flushing
No laxative/cleanse-product use, no recent antibiotics, travel, or sick contacts
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Skin/mucous membranes: dry mucous membranes and poor skin turgor consistent with volume depletion; occasional faint facial flush.
Significant orthostasis: BP drops to 84/54 with HR rising to 124 on standing (volume depletion from large secretory losses).
Neuro/MSK: diffuse proximal muscle weakness from profound hypokalemia; no focal deficit.
Neuro: hyporeflexia consistent with profound hypokalemia; no focal deficit.
Abdomen: soft, non-tender, no rebound/guarding, no palpable mass or organomegaly; normoactive-to-increased bowel sounds.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals (tachycardia, low-normal BP, weight loss).
Elicit the focused history — critically, that the diarrhea persists with fasting — and the laxative/infectious negatives only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted exam to reveal dehydration, orthostasis, and the hypokalemic weakness/hyporeflexia.
Build a differential led by VIPoma/WDHA, distinguishing secretory from osmotic diarrhea and naming the must-not-miss competitors (factitious laxative abuse, carcinoid, gastrinoma).

Order a focused workup — BMP, calcium/magnesium, stool osmotic gap with a fasting test and laxative/infectious screen, fasting VIP, and pancreatic imaging — and read the hypokalemic EKG and normal CXR.

Commit to VIPoma, name the mechanisms of the electrolyte/acid–base picture, and manage with resuscitation, telemetry-guided K/Mg repletion, a somatostatin analog, and surgical/oncologic definitive therapy.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on infectious gastroenteritis and giving empiric antibiotics for a months-long secretory diarrhea
Failing to consider or exclude surreptitious laxative abuse
Repleting potassium without magnesium or without telemetry in profound hypokalemia
Discharging or oral-rehydrating a severely hypokalemic, volume-depleted patient
managementPearls: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation for large secretory losses with strict ins/outs
Urgent careful potassium (with magnesium) repletion on continuous telemetry
Somatostatin analog (octreotide/lanreotide) to suppress VIP and control diarrhea

criticalInterpretationItems: Prominent U waves with flat/inverted T waves, ST depression and apparent long QU on sinus tachycardia — profound hypokalemia (K 2.4) and a torsades substrate.
Normal chest film: clear lungs, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette, no acute process.

Urgent careful potassium (with magnesium) repletion on continuous telemetry

imageInterpretationPearls: Read the EKG against the chemistry — prominent U waves with flat T waves and ST depression are the signature of severe hypokalemia.
Replete magnesium alongside potassium; hypomagnesemia makes hypokalemia refractory and torsades more likely.
A normal CXR is a deliberate pertinent negative that redirects you to a metabolic/secretory cause.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's fear about her fluid losses and weakness
Asks permission before sensitive questions (laxative/cleanse use, eating habits)
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
